coredata.dc:title: Electrodeposited histidine-(CuCr)layered double hydroxides/carbon dots for in-tube solid-phase microextraction of chlorophenols from water, juice, and honey samples followed by HPLC-UV
coredata.dc:creator: Aghaziarati, Mohsen
Yamini, Yadollah
Shamsayei, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: A novel adsorbent consisting of a composition of carbon dots and CuCr-layered double hydroxides intercalated with l-histidine (C-dots@His/LDHs) was introduced. This adsorbent was electrochemically deposited on the inner surface of a capillary copper tube. It was used as an adsorbent for in-tube solid-phase microextraction of chlorophenols (CPs). Separation and measurement of CPs were done by high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet detector. The main parameters which had the most impact on the extraction efficiency and time such as extraction time and flow rate, desorption time and flow rate, ionic strength (salt concentration) and pH were optimized. Calibration curves (0.5–1000 μg L−1) were plotted in real sample (tap water) under optimal conditions which coefficients of determination better than 0.9893 and relative recoveries in the range of 88–120 % were obtained. The limits of detection (S/N = 3) and limits of quantification (S/N = 10) were obtained in the range of 0.1–1.0 μg L−1 and 0.3–3.0 μg L−1, respectively. The intra- and inter-assay precisions (RSD%, n = 3) were better than 5.9 and 8.8 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chlorophenols
Electrodeposition
In-tube solid-phase microextraction
Layered double hydroxides

xocs:item-toc-section-title.ce:italic: Synthesis of C-dots composited with amino acid intercalated LDHs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of extraction flow rate and time
Optimization of desorption flow rate and time
Optimization of type of desorption solvent
Comparison of in-tube solid-phase microextraction with other similar extraction methods

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors appreciate Tarbiat Modares University for its financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Above: FT-IR spectra of Glu, His, and Asp/LDHs and bottom: XRD patterns of His/LDHs, Glu/LDHs, Asp/LDHs and NO3-LDH.
SEM images of NO3-LDHs (A and B) and His/LDHs (C and D) coated on the copper surface in different magnifications.
TEM images of carbon dots (A), LDHs (B) and C-dots@His/LDHs (C).
Chromatograms of 100 μg L−1 spiked blank and non-spiked real samples..
Figures of merit for IT-SPME-HPLC-UV of CPs. a .

head.ce:title: Electrodeposited histidine-(CuCr)layered double hydroxides/carbon dots for in-tube solid-phase microextraction of chlorophenols from water, juice, and honey samples followed by HPLC-UV

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.Tarbiat Modares University, Department of Chemistry, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel adsorbent consisting of a composition of carbon dots and CuCr-layered double hydroxides intercalated with l-histidine (C-dots@His/LDHs) was introduced. This adsorbent was electrochemically deposited on the inner surface of a capillary copper tube. It was used as an adsorbent for in-tube solid-phase microextraction of chlorophenols (CPs). Separation and measurement of CPs were done by high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet detector. The main parameters which had the most impact on the extraction efficiency and time such as extraction time and flow rate, desorption time and flow rate, ionic strength (salt concentration) and pH were optimized. Calibration curves (0.5–1000 μg L−1) were plotted in real sample (tap water) under optimal conditions which coefficients of determination better than 0.9893 and relative recoveries in the range of 88–120 % were obtained. The limits of detection (S/N = 3) and limits of quantification (S/N = 10) were obtained in the range of 0.1–1.0 μg L−1 and 0.3–3.0 μg L−1, respectively. The intra- and inter-assay precisions (RSD%, n = 3) were better than 5.9 and 8.8 %, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, determination and extraction methods are widely used to measure the analytes and pollutants from environmental and food samples [1]. microextraction methods include solid-phase microextraction (SPME), stir-bar sorptive extraction (SBSE), single-drop microextraction (SDME), membrane-assisted liquid–liquid extraction (MALLE), dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction (DLLME), dispersive solid-phase extraction (d-SPE) [2], dispersive micro solid-phase extraction [3]. Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) is progressing rapidly [4] due to its outstanding features such as easy sampling, eco-compatibility, simple extraction, and excellent efficiency [5]. These features have made SPME one of the best choices for analytical applications. SPME methods that use extractant materials with different morphologies such as thin films, nanoparticles, packed tubes, fibers, stir bars, and capillary tubes have grown in number [6]. Among various forms of SPME, in-tube solid phase microextraction (IT-SPME) method has been used extensively due to its easy automation, high performance, miniaturization, reduced organic solvent consumption, good precision and trueness, and connection with analytical tools [7,8].

In this method, the adsorber must be selected in such a way where maximum precision, trueness, and efficiency is obtained. Therefore, the adsorbent plays a key role in the extraction of analytes [9]. SPME coatings such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [10], carbon-based materials, conductive polymers [11], metal oxides, ionic liquids (ILs), and layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have been studied extensively [12–14]. Among all these materials, special attention has been paid to layered double hydroxides and carbon dots because these materials have excellent properties, including low cost, intercalation, high anion capacity, porosity, wide surface area, good efficiency of extraction, and compatibility with environment [15]. The positive charge of LDHs layers are neutralized by placing anions between the layers [16]. LDHs follows the following general formula [17]:
(1)
[
M
1 − x
2 +
M x
3 +
(
O H
)
2
]
x +
[
(
A
n −
)
x n
]
x −
. m
H 2
O
Where
x
is the ratio of trivalent metal (
M
3 +
) to total metals (
M
3 +
M
3 +
+
M
2 +
),
A
n −
is the symbol of the anions that are placed between the layers to neutralize the positive charge of the layers, and the
m
indicates the amount of water between the layers [18]. Changing the interlayer anion is the most usual and easiest method to improve the adsorption properties of LDHs [19]. Various chemical and electrochemical methods have been introduced for the easy synthesis of LDHs [20–22]. In this work, electrochemical synthesis was used for the synthesis of LDHs due to its simplicity, short time, easy control, cost efficiency, and single step [23]. In electrochemical synthesis, a uniform adsorbent with the desired thickness is produced, which adheres well to the surface of the substrate and allows us to use the adsorbent more times.

Carbon dots are another category of nanomaterials that have attracted special attention. The diameter of quasi-spherical carbon-dot particles are less than 10 nm [24]. C-dots are particularly interesting due to their unique properties like easy surface modification, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [25]. Until now, several studies have been conducted to investigate the core and surface characteristics of C-dots. According to the results of these studies, a hybrid nucleus of sp2 and sp3 with hydroxyl, carbonyl, or sulfonate groups is predicted for C-dots. The presence of carboxylic acid or other groups with different percentages of oxygen atoms on C-dots leads to improve the extraction efficiency [26]. There are several ways to prepare C-dots, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, and ultrasound. These approaches split large carbon structures into small nanoscale carbon materials (top-down methods). There are also other ways to synthesize C-dots, such as microwave, thermal decomposition, and hydrothermal treatment (bottom-up methods) [25,27,28]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal method has attracted more attention due to easy synthesis, low cost, and controlled morphology [29]. Based on the published studies, the chlorination process is used to purify water providing a suitable substrate for the presence of CPs [30]. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency, CPs are one of the most toxic, harmful, and carcinogenic contaminants in tap water [30]. Therefore, introducing appropriate methods to determine the trace amounts of chlorophenol in real samples like water, juice and honey are needed.

In this work, the layered double hydroxides were intercalated with three amino acids, including l-histidine (His/LDHs), l-asparagine (Asp/LDHs), and l-glutamine (Glu/LDHs). The extraction efficiencies and stabilities of the synthesized LDHs were compared. Then, the synthesized LDHs was composited with C-dots to improve the extraction efficiency. Using a simple two-electrode system, intercalated LDHs was deposited in the inner walls of a copper tube. The C-dots solution was flown through the tube until it could be adsorbed on the LDHs nanosheets. The experimental data confirmed that C-dots@His/LDHs has higher extraction efficiency than His/LDHs, Asp/LDHs, and Glu/LDHs. Thus, C-dots@His/LDHs was selected as the proper coating. Then, a new C-dots@His/LDHs was utilized for the extraction and measurement of CPs in water, juice, and honey samples. Based on our knowledge, C-dots@His/LDHs has not been reported in the literature as an IT-SPME adsorbent so far.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. 4-Chlorophenol (99 %) (4-CP), 2,3-dichlorophenol (99 %) (2,3-DCP), 2,4-dichlorophenole (99 %) (2,4- DCP), 3,5-dichlorophenol (99 %) (3,5-DCP), 2,3,5-trichlorophenol (99 %) (2,3,5-TCP), and 2,3,5,6-tetrachlorophenol (99 %) (2,3,5,6-TeCP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Standard CPs solutions (1000 mg L−1 each) were prepared by dissolving the compounds in methanol and were stored at 4 °C for a week.

Daily, dilution of standards (1000 mg L−1) in methanol was used to prepare mixed solutions with lower concentrations (0.1–100 mg L−1). Cu(NO3)2.3H2O (99 %), Cr(NO3)3.9H2O (99.99 %), l-histidine (99 %), l-asparagine (99 %) and l-glutamine (99 %), citric acid (99 %), urea (99 %), ethanol (95 %) (EtOH), acetonitrile (99 %) (ACN), acetone (99.5 %) and methanol (99 %) (MeOH) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis MO, USA).

Chromatographic analysis was carried on a Varian 9012 HPLC (Walnut Creek, CA, USA) equipped with a Varian 9050 UV–Vis detector (at λ = 210 nm), a column (ODS-3, 250 mm × 4.6 mm, with a 5-μm particle size) from Hector Company (Daejeon, Korea) and a 20 μL loop. Also, an isocratic elution used with a combination of 70:30 ACN and phosphate buffer (pH = 4 and 5 mM) as mobile phase at the flow rate of 1 mL min−1. The separations were performed at room temperature.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) carried on Philips (Eindhoven, Netherland) diffractometer and patterns were taken with Co Kα radiation (1.78901 Å) between 3° and 80° generated at 40 kV and 30 mA at room temperature to characterize the structure of the LDHs. The morphology of materials was determined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) model MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The substitution of C-dots on the LDHs was confirmed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) on a 200 kV Schottky transmission electron microscopy from Zeiss Microscopy (Baden-Württemberg, Germany). The FT-IR spectra of the samples were recorded on Thermos Scientific Nicolet IR100 (Madison, WI, USA) with the KBr pellet in the wave number range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

In a typical experiment, an amino acid-intercalated LDHs compound was synthesized by a two-electrode system. A platinum rode was used as the counter electrode, and a copper tube was applied as working electrode. By applying electrical potential, the adsorbent was synthesized on the inner surface of copper tube.

Before beginning the synthesis procedure, the copper tube with a length of 100 mm and an inner diameter of 0.8 mm was washed with MeOH and ultrapure water respectively and was air-dried at room temperature. Then, a mixture of Cu(NO3)2.3H2O, Cr(NO3)3.9H2O, and amino acid was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water at room temperature to optimize the reaction voltage, reaction time, and the molar ratios of (Cu2+ + Cr3+):amino acid. In addition, the cathodic voltage effect (−3.0, −4.0, −5.0, and – 6.0 V) in the reaction procedure at different time intervals (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 min) was studied. According to the obtained data, the optimal parameters are −5 V, 30 min, and the ratio of (Cu2+ + Cr3+):His is (3 + 1):9. Therefore, the certain amounts of 0.36 g (0.09 mol L−1) of Cu(NO3)2.3H2O, 0.2 g (0.03 mol L−1) of Cr(NO3)3.9H2O, and 0.8 g (0.26 mol L−1) of histidine were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. Afterward, the electrochemical reaction was started by passing the obtained solution through the copper tube by a peristaltic pump and applying the cathodic voltage to the copper tube simultaneously. When the electrochemical reaction finished, the tube was washed with MeOH and water, respectively, and was dried in an oven at 60 °C under atmospheric conditions for 4 h.

At this stage, carbon dots was synthesized using the methods suggested in the literature with slight modifications [31]. Briefly, 4.0 g of citric acid and 2.0 g of urea were dissolved in 100 mL ultrapure water. The obtained solution was heated at 160 °C for 12 h in an autoclave. When the reaction was completed, the product was cooled down to room temperature in the vicinity of air and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Synthesis of C-dots composited with amino acid intercalated LDHs

ce:section.ce:para: Fabrication of the C-dots-amino acid intercalated LDHs composite was done by passing C-dots through a copper tube for 4 h by a peristaltic pump to stabilize the C-dots on amino acid/LDHs. Afterward, the adsorbent was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h and then washed with ultrapure water.

The solution containing analytes was passed through the adsorbent using a peristaltic pump for 15 min (adsorption step). 200 μL of MeOH was passed through the tube for 15 min to desorb the analytes (desorption step). Finally, the desorption solvent was injected into the HPLC-UV for analysis.

Extraction of CPs from the real samples was studied by selecting four water samples (tap and well) from Kudzar (Markazi, Iran), home-made orange and peach juices, and one honey sample from a local market (branded Gozal) in 2023. 2.0 g of honey sample was accurately weighed and dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water at room temperature. Then 3.0 g NaCl was added and filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the suspended particles and finally reached to 20 mL with ultrapure water under vigorous stirring. 3.0 g NaCl was added to 20 mL water samples, then filtered by 0.22 μm membrane filter. 3.0 g NaCl was added to 10 mL of each juice sample and filtered by 0.45 and then 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove pulps. After vigorously shaking the solution, its volume was reached 20 mL by ultrapure water.

Various adsorbents can be used in the SPME, including PDMS and conductive polymers. These materials are not very useable due to limitations such as low stability, low selectivity, high price, high toxicity, and difficulty in preparation. In this work, a high-performance adsorbent has been introduced with unique features. Choosing a powerful adsorbent can have a significant effect on selectivity and even the limit of detection. LDHs due to its features such as high anion exchange capacity, low price, high extraction efficiency, high surface area, environmentally friendliness, and good yield of synthesis, can be a very suitable option as an adsorbent for extraction of CPs. The NO3-LDHs does not adhere well to the surface of the copper tube and is removed from the surface with washing. Therefore, composition with conductive polymers like polythiophene [25] and polyaniline [26] were done to increase both their stability and extraction efficiency. The vital point to consider when choosing a suitable intercalate is that the material must have several functional groups to have π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding to be able to increase the extraction efficiency. Therefore, l-histidine, l-asparagine, and l-glutamine were selected. On the other hand, different metals were used to optimize the metal hydroxides that make up the LDHs layers. Because by changing the type of metals, the stability and morphology will vary. His/CuCr-LDHs, His/MgAl-LDHs, His/CuAl-LDHs, His/MgCr-LDHs, His/ZnCr-LDHs, and His/NiAl-LDHs were synthesized. The results showed that His/CuCr-LDHs was the best adsorbent in terms of extraction efficiency (Fig. S1). Therefore, the C-dots@His/LDHs coating was used in the subsequent experiments.

The crystalline structure of the adsorbent was investigated using the XRD. Using this method, materials show diffraction peaks in different 2θs that leads to identify them. According to JCPDS file 00–0350965, (003), (006), (009), (015) and (018) are diffraction peaks for CuCr-LDHs [32]. For LDHs, the basal space length (thickness of sheets plus interlamellar length) is obtained using the 2θ of (003) and Bragg's equation. Since the thickness of the LDHs is 4.8 nm, a simple calculation gives the length of the interlamellar space where the amino acid is located. Amino acid molecule is larger than the nitrate ion, so the distance between the layers must be increased when the amino acid goes through the interlamellar space instead of nitrate ion. The calculations and results confirm this fact. The results are shown in Table S1 and the corresponding pattern in Fig. 1 . Furthermore, from the above result, and the sharp and symmetric diffraction peaks, it was understood that His/LDHs has been successfully synthesized and has good crystallinity. Meanwhile, no other crystalline phase was observed, which indicates that the His/LDHs with high purity was synthesized on the copper substrate. Detection of functional groups is one of the best tools that we can use to investigate the truth of synthesis; therefore, FT-IR was used (Fig. 1). The appearance of the peaks 3424, 1627, 1380, 500–850 cm−1 corresponding to the O–H stretching, O–H bending, NO3 −, M-O-M, O-M-O, and O-M vibrations, and the peaks 1631, 1201, 1272, 1419, 1519, 2840 and 1751 cm−1 corresponding to the CO, C–O, C–N, N–CN, CN, carboxylic acid O–H stretching and CC vibrations, respectively, shows that the synthesis of His/LDHs has been done well. On the other hand, the peak related to nitrate is reduced compared to other LDHs peaks, which is due to replacement with histidine [33]. The FE-SEM technique was used to study the morphology and size of pores of C-dots@His/LDHs. Fig. 2 A, B, C and D are pictures of His/LDHs with different magnifications. The uniform synthesis of the adsorbent on the copper tube is clearly defined that the electrodeposition technique was able to produce a uniform, porous adsorbent grown in three dimensions. Also, by investigating the morphology of the His/LDHs and NO3-LDHs, it can be seen that His/LDHs is the suitable option for extraction of CPs, and the changes in the morphology of the His/LDHs demonstrate the successful intercalation of l-histidine into the LDHs compound. TEM is one of the best ways to detect the presence of C-dots on LDHs sheets. Fig. 3 A and B shows the C-dots and LDHs, respectively and c-dots on LDHs are presented in Fig. 3C.

In this study, the main extraction parameters of CPs by IT-SPME coupled with HPLC-UV (the pH of the sample solution, salt effect, extraction time, desorption time, desorption solvents, extraction flow rate, and desorption flow rate) were investigated and optimized. Optimization was carried out by one-variable-at-a-time method using 100 μg L−1 mixed standard solution of CPs. The relevant data and figures are in the Electronic Supporting Material (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). The summary of the results is as follows: (a) concentration of salt: 15 % of NaCl; (b) optimum pH: 7.0; (c) extraction flow rate: 2 mL min−1; (d) extraction time: 15 min; (e) desorption solvent: MeOH; (f) desorption time: 15 min; and (g) desorption flow rate: 2.0 mL min−1.

The ionic strength of solution is one of the factors affecting the extraction efficiency, so, optimization of it can increase the extraction efficiency (Fig. S2A). Therefore, the extraction was done under NaCl concentrations of 0 %, 5 %, 10 %, 15 % and 20 %, and it was observed that when the concentration is 15 %, the extraction efficiency increases due to the salting-out effect but when the concentration is over 15 %, extraction efficiency reduces due to increasing viscosity of the solution and reduction of analyte penetration in to the adsorbent. Also, when the Cl− concentration in the solution increases, there is a competition between the analyte and Cl− to penetrate into the adsorbent, which reduces the extraction efficiency [8,34].

Adjusting the pH is one of the most important optimization steps. Since LDHs have hydroxyl groups, the acidic or basic environment can affect the extraction efficiency. It was observed that the adsorbent is destroyed in acidic environments, also, in basic environments due to the conversion of hydroxyl groups into O−, negatively charged l-histidine leaves the adsorbent and the structure of the adsorbent collapses and changes to metal oxide. Therefore, the extraction was done in pH of 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 and pH = 7 was selected as the optimum pH (Fig. S2B) [35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of extraction flow rate and time
ce:section.ce:para: In the extraction process, it is necessary for the analyte to have enough time to enter the adsorbent, and the fresh solution must be continuously exposed to the adsorbent. On the other hand, the waste of time in extraction should be avoided, so the time and extraction flow rate should be optimized. Therefore the extraction flow rate and time were optimized separately. As a result (Figs. S2C and D), among 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mL min−1 for extraction flow rate, 2 mL min−1 was chosen as the optimal flow rate, and among 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 min for extraction time, 15 min was chosen as the optimal time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of desorption flow rate and time
ce:section.ce:para: In the desorption process, it is necessary to optimize the time and flow rate so that the maximum amount of efficiency occurs and to avoid wasting time. Therefore, the desorption process was optimized at times of 5, 10, 15, and 20 min and at flow rates of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mL min−1 separately, and 15 min and 2 mL min−1 were obtained for the time and desorption flow rate, respectively (Figs. S3A and B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of type of desorption solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Determining the type of solvent for desorption is important because the type of solvent affects the adsorbent and extraction efficiency. For this purpose, a comparison was made between four solvents, ACN, acetone, MeOH and EtOH. The data obtained from the peak areas (Fig. S3C) showed that MeOH was the best choice due to higher extraction efficiency among considered solvents. Therefore, MeOH was chosen as the desorption solvent.

The copper tube in which the adsorbent was electrodeposited was used for several extractions. The tube was washed with MeOH after each extraction to reduce the memory effect. This step was considered as a cleaning stage before the following extraction procedure. After the cleaning step, the memory effect was 0.2 %. This value cannot considerably change the amount of extraction. The excellent performance of the adsorbent can be due to the porous morphology and substrate characteristics. To determine its reusability, the adsorbent was applied in 100 SPMEs of CPs. The data confirmed that no observable change in extraction efficiency was found. The extraction data revealed high mechanical stability, high reusability, and low memory effect for the prepared adsorbent. It should be noted that all extractions (after the optimization steps) were done by only one coated tube.

The values in Table 1 including limit of detection (LOD), linear dynamic range (LDR), and intra- and inter-assay precision (RSD%) have been provided to assess the performance of IT-SPME. Mix standard solutions of CPs were prepared to evaluate the linearity of the method. Since, most of the real samples were water and in the initial investigation, tap water did not have any contamination, it was selected as a blank sample and the calibration curves were plotted in tap water. As shown in Fig. S4, the linear range of the method was 3.0–1000 μg L−1 for 4-CP, 2.0–1000 μg L−1 for 2,3-DCP and 2,4-DCP, and 0.5–1000 μg L−1 for 3,5-DCP, 2,3,5-TCP, and 2,3,5,6-TeCP with a coefficient of determination better than 0.9826 (calibration plots are shown in Fig. S4). To measure the LODs, the blank solution was injected ten times and then the signal intensity to noise ratio was measured in the time range of the desired analyte peak. The standard deviation of these ten repetitions (S/N) was calculated and 3 × S/N was considered as the LOD of the method. The LODs (at S/N = 3) were 1.0 μg L−1 for 4-CP, 0.5 μg L−1 for 2,3-DCP and 2,4-DCP, and 0.1 μg L−1 for 3,5-DCP, 2,3,5-TCP, and 2,3,5,6-TeCP. The precision of the method (RSD%) was evaluated by three replicates of samples with certain concentrations (10, 50, 100 μg L−1), which were obtained between 4.5 and 8.8 % for inter-day and between 4.2 and 5.9 % for intra-day. From the analysis of the results, it can be concluded that the IT-SPME method has good reproducibility for the extraction of CPs, and the electrodeposition technique is capable to produce a uniform and reproducible adsorbent.

There are several methods for controlling the risk levels of chemicals and analyzing methods and checking their compliance with the principles of green chemistry, including AEC, complex GAPI and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) approaches, whose results are usually consistent [37]. In this work, AGREE method was used for investigation. The AGREE approach gives a complete and straightforward evaluation of greenness based on the twelve principles of green chemistry (SIGNIFICANCE) on a scale of 0–1. The result is a pictogram that shows the final score and performance of the method in each criterion separately [38]. This work was approved as a green analytical method by achieving 0.57 out of 1.0 as shown in Fig. S5.

The applicability of the IT-SPME method was investigated in honey, juice and water as real samples. One can refer to “Preparation of real samples” section to see how real samples were prepared. By this method the presence of several numbers of CPs in real samples was confirmed, and the results of the measurements are shown in Table 2 . The following relative recovery (RR%) equation defines the trueness of the method after spiking 20.0 and 100 μg L−1 of mixed standard solution to the real samples:
(2)
R R % =
[
C
e x t r a c t e d
−
C
i n i t i a l
C
a d d e d
]
× 100
Where the initial concentration of the analytes in the real samples, detected concentration of the analytes after extraction, and the concentration of standards spiked into the real samples are indicated by Cinitial, Cextracted, and Cadded, respectively. The results demonstrated acceptable recoveries in the range of 88–120 % for the CPs, which is due to the relatively small effect of the real matrices on the extraction efficiency. The relative standard deviation (RSD%) after three replicates in each expected concentration was considered the method’s precision. The RSD% values for the determination of the CPs in the real samples were located in the range of 3.3–8.0 %. Fig. 4 shows the chromatograms of spiked blank and non-spiked real samples. The results indicate that the present method takes advantage of good precision, performance and trueness for the determination of CPs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of in-tube solid-phase microextraction with other similar extraction methods
ce:section.ce:para: The results of different extraction and measurement methods of CPs are collected in Table 3 and compared with IT-SPME in number of analytes, time of analysis, LOD, LOQ, LDR, RSD%, relative recoveries (RR%), number of real samples and coefficients of determination [39–48]. According to Table 3 and it can be seen that the relative recoveries of 88–120 are very favorable compared to other methods, and 6 CPs have been separated and measured simultaneously in just 11 min, which is better than other reports. The RSDs% of less than 8.4 shows the high repeatability of IT-SPME, and finally this method and its adsorbent were used in water and complex matrices such as honey and juice samples. In addition, they produced low LODs (0.1–1.0 μg L−1) and LOQs (0.3–3.0 μg L−1). IT-SPME and C-dots@His/LDHs meet the requirements for the determination of CPs in complex matrices. There are two reasons for these advantages: (a) C-dots@His/LDHs has a good potential for the extraction of CPs due to the water-resistant structure and large basal spacing (in comparison with non-intercalated LDHs), and (b) the trueness and time of the analysis were significantly optimized. As compared with other methods, the present method shows some benefits such as: (1) good recovery and low matrix effect, which is appropriate for the analysis of CPs in different samples; (2) good mechanical and chemical stability and high reusability of the C-dots@His/LDHs coating; (3) the cheap and environmentally friendly C-dots@His/LDHs adsorbent; and (4) producing low organic waste solutions. However, complex matrices cannot be directly analyzed by this method, which is known as one of the limitations of this method. Also, this method is not a selective extraction method for the target analytes. Therefore, the introduction of new SPME methods is necessary to overcome the limitations of the method.

Here, a novel l-histidine-intercalated-(CuCr)layered double hydroxides was deposited in the inner walls of a capillary copper tube using electrochemically method. Then, it was composited with carbon dots (C-dots@His/LDHs). The adsorbent is an appropriate selection for extraction and preconcentration of CPs. The adsorbent demonstrated good features like easy synthesis, low cost, high extraction capacity, low memory effect, high chemical stability, high reusability, and resistance in water compared to existing commercial adsorbents. As far as we know, for the first time, C-dots@His/LDHs has been used as IT-SPME coating to preconcentrate and determine ultra-trace amounts of CPs. It can be concluded that the C-dots@His/LDHs paired with IT-SPME showed excellent trueness, repeatability, and easy operation. In addition, a wide linear range, low limit of detection, and high recovery are the other strengths of the method. This method was successfully used to extract and determine CPs from honey, juice and water samples.

Mohsen Aghaziarati: Conceptualization; Data curation; Investigation; Methodology; Validation; Writing – original draft. Yadollah Yamini: Funding acquisition; Project administration; Writing – review & editing. Maryam Shamsayei: Data curation; Methodology Structured.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors appreciate Tarbiat Modares University for its financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
